summary: Jeff Atwood explores the wide skill gap between programmers by sharing an anecdote about a developer named 'Stinky' who didn't understand basic boolean negation. The post humorously suggests that IDE tools like Clippy could help struggling developers by detecting problematic code patterns in real-time. Atwood uses this as a jumping point to discuss VS.NET 2005's customizable exception intellisense, showing how developers can improve the default error messages. The underlying message balances humor with a genuine observation that development tools could do more to guide less experienced programmers.
key_points: Some programmers lack fundamental programming knowledge like boolean logic, yet still work as professional developers
IDEs could theoretically detect poor coding patterns and offer guidance in real-time, similar to Clippy
VS.NET 2005 allows customization of exception intellisense through XML editing to provide more helpful debugging messages
The industry joke about Clippy masks a real opportunity: proactive IDE assistance for common mistakes
There's a gap between formal CS education and practical programming skills that tools might help bridge
money_quotes: Even the best programmers make shitty software, with bugs. But some programmers are naturally proficient at creating this special kind of software.
To hell with education, your degree in computer science and the tons of books you read. You don't need any of that to be a champion developer like Stinky.
Just learn the copy-paste method, remember all the properties of Janus Gridex and ActiveBar control, and the world is yours.
After Bojan recovered from the shock of realizing that Stinky didn't know the basics of logical algebra...
It looks like you're writing a for loop!
key_insight: Development tools could do more to actively guide programmers away from fundamental mistakes, turning the much-maligned Clippy concept into genuinely useful real-time code assistance.
